title: Hard-coded secrets are security-sensitive
description: This rule detects variables/fields/properties having a name matching a list of words (secret, token, credential, auth, api[_.-]?key) being assigned a pseudorandom hard-coded value. The pseudorandomness of the hard-coded value is based on its entropy and the probability to be human-readable. The randomness sensibility can be adjusted if needed.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
const string mySecret = "EXAMPLE_SECRET_HASH"; // Noncompliant
const string apiKey = "FAKE_API_KEY_EXAMPLE"; // Noncompliant
private string authToken = "FAKE_JWT_TOKEN_EXAMPLE"; // Noncompliant
compliant:
string mySecret = Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("MY_SECRET"); // Compliant
string apiKey = ConfigurationManager.AppSettings["ApiKey"]; // Compliant
private string authToken = GetSecretFromSecureStore("auth_token"); // Compliant

logic.detection_conditions: Detect assignment statements where secret-related variable names (secret, token, credential, auth, apikey, api_key) are assigned high-entropy string literals (long alphanumeric strings, JWT tokens, hex hashes)
logic.triggering_conditions: (Variable name matches secret patterns) AND (Assigned value is high-entropy string literal) AND (NOT in excluded contexts)
exclusion_conditions: Property/constant names that are configuration keys
never_trigger_on: Method calls that retrieve secrets (GetSecret*, Retrieve*)
logic.reasoning: This rule detects hard-coded secrets by identifying assignment statements where secret-related variable names are assigned high-entropy string literals. High entropy is approximated by requiring strings of 20+ alphanumeric characters, which matches typical API keys, tokens, hashes, and JWT tokens. Excludes configuration retrieval methods, method calls, simple property names, and short strings that are unlikely to be real secrets.
